questions.prompt: When should you use the `prose` class?

choices.text:
Rich-text content where you don't control the HTML — Markdown rendered to HTML, CMS-authored content, blog posts, documentation, terms of service. `prose` automatically styles nested `

`, headings, lists, code blocks, blockquotes, links with consistent typography, spacing, and line heights. Don't use for UI text (buttons, form labels, navigation) — those should use Tailwind's font utilities directly (`text-lg font-semibold` etc.). Pair `prose` with `dark:prose-invert` for dark mode and customize per-element with `prose-headings:font-display prose-a:text-blue-600` modifiers.

questions.explanation: prose for rich-text where you don't control the HTML (Markdown, CMS). Not for UI text.

questions.prompt: Large hero heading looks loose and awkward at 6xl size. Common fix?

choices.text: Add `tracking-tight` (negative letter-spacing) and `leading-tight` or `leading-none` (reduced line-height). Default line-heights are tuned for body text; at display sizes (4xl+), they read as too loose. Combination: `text-6xl font-bold tracking-tight leading-tight`. For hero text where you want cinematic visual rhythm, `leading-none` plus generous tracking adjustment. Small adjustments to tracking and leading make a disproportionate visual difference at large sizes; same headline with default vs tuned typography reads as amateur vs professional.

questions.explanation: tracking-tight + leading-tight (or leading-none) for display sizes. Disproportionate visual impact.
